concepts.definition: The ability of an actor to influence others and achieve desired outcomes. Can be hard power (military/economic coercion) or soft power (attraction through culture, values, policies).
A state's supreme authority over its territory and domestic affairs, free from external interference. Core principle of the Westphalian system established in 1648.
The rightfulness and acceptance of power or authority. Can be derived from democratic elections, tradition, performance, or international recognition.
Mutual reliance among actors through trade, finance, communication, and institutions. Creates both cooperation opportunities and vulnerability to external shocks.

theories.name: Realism
theories.summary: Views states as rational actors in an anarchic international system pursuing survival and power. Conflict is inevitable; cooperation is limited and temporary. Key thinkers: Thucydides, Machiavelli, Morgenthau, Waltz.

theories.name: Liberalism
theories.summary: Argues cooperation is possible through international institutions, trade, democracy, and law. Interdependence creates incentives for peace. Key thinkers: Kant, Wilson, Keohane, Nye.

theories.name: Constructivism
theories.summary: Emphasizes that norms, identities, ideas, and culture shape state interests and behavior. The international system is socially constructed, not fixed. Key thinkers: Wendt, Finnemore, Katzenstein.

powers.note: Global military reach, NATO leader, economic hegemon. Faces challenges from rising China and domestic polarization.
Rising power with Belt and Road Initiative, Indo-Pacific focus. Tensions over Taiwan, South China Sea, trade.
Economic giant, regulatory superpower. Challenges: energy security, unity, defense coordination.
Regional military power with nuclear arsenal. Ukraine war has reshaped European security landscape.
Fastest-growing major economy, rising diplomatic influence. Border tensions with China and Pakistan.
Economic power with expanding defense role. Key US ally in Asia-Pacific balancing against China.

conflicts.name: Russia–Ukraine War
conflicts.note: Major European security crisis since 2014, full-scale invasion February 2022. Projected 28,300 battle deaths in 2026. Reshaped NATO, European energy policy, and global food security.

conflicts.name: Israel–Palestine (Gaza)
conflicts.note: Deep dispute over land, security, and recognition. October 2023 escalation caused massive humanitarian crisis. Projected 7,700 deaths in 2026. Central to Middle East stability.

conflicts.name: Sudan Civil War
conflicts.note: Power struggle between RSF and SAF since April 2023. Severe humanitarian crisis with 10+ million displaced. Projected 4,300 deaths in 2026. Regional instability threat.

conflicts.name: Myanmar Civil War
conflicts.note: Military coup February 2021 triggered nationwide resistance. Ethnic armed groups vs. military junta. Major humanitarian crisis with limited international response.

conflicts.name: Taiwan Strait Tensions
conflicts.note: Rising tensions between China and US-aligned actors over Taiwan's status. Most dangerous potential conflict zone. Critical for global semiconductor supply chains.

conflicts.name: Syria
conflicts.note: Civil war since 2011 involving Assad regime, rebels, ISIS remnants, Kurdish forces, Turkey, Russia, Iran. 500,000+ deaths. Massive refugee crisis affecting Europe and Middle East.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Electoral democracy with strong institutions, federal systems (USA, Canada), multi-party competition. Politics dominated by campaigns, lobbying, and media influence. Presidential (USA) vs parliamentary (Canada) systems.
worldOverview.challenges: US democratic backsliding under Trump administration, polarization, immigration debates, trade tensions with China, USMCA implementation.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Presidential systems with weak institutions. Populism common (left and right). Frequent constitutional crises, military involvement in politics. Strong informal networks and clientelism. Social movements powerful.
worldOverview.challenges: Venezuela humanitarian crisis, Nicaragua autocracy, corruption, inequality, climate migration, Chinese economic influence, US intervention legacy.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Parliamentary democracies, coalition governments, strong welfare states, multi-party systems. EU supranational governance. Consensus-building culture in North, more polarized in East. High civic participation.
worldOverview.challenges: Ukraine war, energy crisis, migration, far-right rise, EU unity, Russian hybrid warfare, economic stagnation, demographic decline.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: China: One-party authoritarian capitalism, surveillance state, social credit. Japan/South Korea: Liberal democracies with dominant parties. North Korea: Totalitarian hereditary dictatorship. Strong state bureaucracies, economic nationalism.
worldOverview.challenges: Taiwan invasion risk, North Korea nukes, US-China rivalry, South China Sea disputes, aging populations, economic slowdown in China.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Monarchies (Gulf states), military regimes (Egypt, Syria), theocracy (Iran), hybrid (Turkey). Patronage networks, tribal politics, religious authority. Weak civil society, limited political freedoms. Oil wealth shapes governance.
worldOverview.challenges: Israeli-Palestinian conflict, Iran nuclear program, Syrian war, Yemen crisis, Saudi-Iran rivalry, ISIS remnants, water scarcity, youth unemployment.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Presidential systems with weak institutions. Neopatrimonialism: personal rule through patronage. Ethnic-based politics. Military coups frequent (Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso). Election rigging common. Strong informal power structures.
worldOverview.challenges: Sudan civil war, Ethiopian conflicts, DRC instability, jihadist insurgencies in Sahel, coups, climate disasters, Chinese debt, youth bulge, corruption.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: India: Electoral democracy with Hindu nationalist trend under Modi, populist appeals. Pakistan: Hybrid regime with military influence. Bangladesh: Autocratizing democracy. Politics shaped by religion, caste, ethnicity. Dynastic politics common.
worldOverview.challenges: India-Pakistan tensions, Kashmir conflict, Bangladesh autocracy, Afghanistan instability, climate vulnerability, water conflicts, religious nationalism.

worldOverview.politicsStyle: Russia: Authoritarian personalist regime (Putin), managed democracy, siloviki (security services) dominance, propaganda control. Central Asia: Post-Soviet autocracies with presidential cults, clan politics, security apparatus control.
worldOverview.challenges: Ukraine war consequences, economic sanctions on Russia, succession uncertainty, Chinese influence in Central Asia, ethnic tensions, resource dependence.

historicalCrises.name: Great Depression
historicalCrises.description: Worst economic collapse of 20th century. Wall Street crash 1929 triggered global recession with 25% unemployment in US, bank failures, poverty. Led to political extremism.
historicalCrises.actors: USA (Hoover, FDR), Germany (Weimar Republic collapse), UK, France, global banking system
historicalCrises.approach: USA: New Deal (Keynesian stimulus, welfare state, regulations). Germany: Turn to Nazism, autarky. UK: Protectionism. International cooperation failed.
historicalCrises.impact: Rise of fascism, WWII roots, Keynesian economics adopted, welfare states created, Bretton Woods system established post-war.

historicalCrises.name: World War II
historicalCrises.description: Deadliest conflict in history: 70-85 million deaths. Nazi Germany's expansion, Holocaust, Pacific War. Total war involving civilians, genocide, nuclear weapons.
historicalCrises.actors: Axis (Germany/Hitler, Japan/Hirohito, Italy/Mussolini) vs Allies (USA/FDR-Truman, UK/Churchill, USSR/Stalin, France)
historicalCrises.approach: Germany: Lebensraum expansion, racial ideology, totalitarian mobilization. Allies: Appeasement failed, total war, strategic bombing, Manhattan Project. USSR: Scorched earth.
historicalCrises.impact: UN created, Cold War began, decolonization wave, NATO/Warsaw Pact, nuclear age, Holocaust trauma, human rights norms, US-Soviet bipolarity.

historicalCrises.name: Cold War (Cuban Missile Crisis peak)
historicalCrises.description: US-Soviet rivalry defined global politics. Proxy wars, arms race, ideological competition. Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) nearly caused nuclear war. Détente periods alternated with tensions.
historicalCrises.actors: USA (Truman-Reagan-Bush Sr.), USSR (Stalin-Khrushchev-Gorbachev), Non-Aligned Movement, NATO, Warsaw Pact
historicalCrises.approach: USA: Containment doctrine, Marshall Plan, military alliances, covert operations. USSR: Communist expansion, support for liberation movements. Both: Nuclear deterrence (MAD), proxy wars.
historicalCrises.impact: Proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan), space race, nuclear proliferation, Third World independence movements, eventual Soviet collapse, US unipolar moment.

historicalCrises.name: Decolonization Wave
historicalCrises.description: Collapse of European empires post-WWII. 100+ new states gained independence in Africa, Asia, Middle East. Often violent transitions, arbitrary borders created conflicts.
historicalCrises.actors: UK, France, Belgium, Portugal (colonizers); India (Nehru, Gandhi), Ghana (Nkrumah), Algeria (FLN), Indonesia (Sukarno), Vietnam (Ho Chi Minh)
historicalCrises.approach: Colonizers: Gradual withdrawal (UK) vs violent resistance (France in Algeria). Independence movements: Armed struggle, non-violence, negotiation. Cold War powers competed for influence.
historicalCrises.impact: New nation-states, Non-Aligned Movement, artificial borders fueled conflicts, neo-colonialism debates, North-South divide, development challenges.

historicalCrises.name: Oil Crisis (OPEC Embargo)
historicalCrises.description: OPEC oil embargo against West for supporting Israel in Yom Kippur War. Oil prices quadrupled, causing stagflation in developed economies. Revealed Western energy vulnerability.
historicalCrises.actors: OPEC (Saudi Arabia, Iran, UAE, Kuwait), USA (Nixon), Israel, Arab states
historicalCrises.approach: OPEC: Oil as political weapon, production cuts. USA: Diplomatic engagement, strategic reserves, fuel efficiency standards. West: Economic recession, inflation, search for alternative energy.
historicalCrises.impact: Stagflation crisis, OPEC power demonstrated, petrodollar system, Western energy policy shifts, Middle East leverage increased, Japanese car industry rise.

historicalCrises.name: Fall of Soviet Union
historicalCrises.description: Communist bloc collapsed. Berlin Wall fell 1989, USSR dissolved 1991. Peaceful end to Cold War. 15 new states emerged from Soviet Union.
historicalCrises.actors: USSR (Gorbachev), Russia (Yeltsin), Eastern European dissidents (Walesa, Havel), USA (Reagan, Bush Sr.), Germany (Kohl)
historicalCrises.approach: Gorbachev: Glasnost (openness), perestroika (restructuring), refused military intervention. Eastern Europe: Peaceful revolutions (except Romania). West: Encouraged reform, German reunification.
historicalCrises.impact: End of Cold War, US sole superpower, NATO expansion, EU enlargement, liberal democracy's 'end of history' moment, Russian economic collapse, ethnic conflicts (Yugoslavia).

historicalCrises.name: 9/11 Terrorist Attacks
historicalCrises.description: Al-Qaeda terrorists hijacked planes, attacking World Trade Center and Pentagon. 3,000 deaths. Redefined US foreign policy, launched 'War on Terror'.
historicalCrises.actors: USA (Bush Jr.), Al-Qaeda (Osama bin Laden), Afghanistan (Taliban), NATO allies
historicalCrises.approach: USA: Invasion of Afghanistan (2001), Iraq War (2003), Patriot Act surveillance, drone warfare, Guantanamo detention. Coalition of the Willing. NATO Article 5 invoked.
historicalCrises.impact: 20-year Afghanistan war, Iraq War chaos, ISIS emergence, erosion of civil liberties, Islamophobia rise, destabilized Middle East, drone warfare normalized, trillions spent.

historicalCrises.name: Global Financial Crisis
historicalCrises.description: US housing bubble burst triggered global recession. Lehman Brothers collapsed. Banking crisis spread worldwide. Worst economic crisis since Great Depression.
historicalCrises.actors: USA (Bush Jr., Obama), Federal Reserve (Bernanke), Wall Street banks, European Central Bank, G20
historicalCrises.approach: USA: Bank bailouts (TARP), stimulus packages, quantitative easing. Europe: Austerity measures. G20 coordination. Dodd-Frank regulations (later weakened).
historicalCrises.impact: Great Recession, sovereign debt crisis in Europe, populist backlash, Occupy Wall Street, mistrust of elites, rise of anti-establishment politics, wealth inequality increased.

historicalCrises.name: Arab Spring
historicalCrises.description: Pro-democracy uprisings across Arab world. Tunisia's Ben Ali overthrown, Egypt's Mubarak fell, Libya's Gaddafi killed, Syria descended into civil war. Hope turned to chaos.
historicalCrises.actors: Tunisia, Egypt (Mubarak, then military), Libya (Gaddafi), Syria (Assad), Yemen, Bahrain. USA (Obama), Gulf monarchies, Russia
historicalCrises.approach: Protesters: Social media mobilization, mass demonstrations. Regimes: Repression (Syria, Bahrain) or concessions (Tunisia). West: Mixed support. Russia/Iran backed Assad.
historicalCrises.impact: Only Tunisia democratized. Syria civil war (500K+ deaths). Libya state collapse. ISIS rise. Refugee crisis to Europe. Authoritarian resilience demonstrated. Democratic hopes crushed.

historicalCrises.name: COVID-19 Pandemic
historicalCrises.description: Global pandemic originating in China. 7+ million official deaths worldwide. Lockdowns, economic collapse, vaccine development race. Exposed global healthcare weaknesses.
historicalCrises.actors: WHO, China (Xi), USA (Trump, Biden), EU, vaccine companies (Pfizer, Moderna), developing countries
historicalCrises.approach: China: Strict lockdowns, contact tracing, censorship. West: Varied responses (Sweden vs Italy). Operation Warp Speed (vaccines). Vaccine nationalism. Misinformation spread.
historicalCrises.impact: Massive economic recession, supply chain disruptions, remote work revolution, vaccine inequity, populist governments struggled, China's model debated, mental health crisis, inflation.

historicalCrises.name: Russia Invades Ukraine
historicalCrises.description: Russia's full-scale invasion February 24, 2022. Largest European war since WWII. Ukrainian resistance surprised world. Reshaping European security order.
historicalCrises.actors: Russia (Putin), Ukraine (Zelensky), NATO (USA/Biden, UK, Poland), EU, China (neutral)
historicalCrises.approach: Russia: Blitzkrieg failed, attrition warfare, war crimes. Ukraine: Total mobilization, Western weapons. West: Sanctions, military aid, energy pivot. Russia-China alignment deepened.
historicalCrises.impact: NATO revitalized, Finland/Sweden joined, European rearmament, energy crisis, global food insecurity, nuclear threats, Russia isolated, authoritarian-democratic divide sharpened.

historicalCrises.name: US Democratic Crisis (Trump Era)
historicalCrises.description: Trump presidency (2017-2021, 2025-present) challenged democratic norms. January 6 Capitol attack (2021). 2024 reelection despite legal issues. Polarization extreme.
historicalCrises.actors: USA (Trump, Biden, Republican Party, Democrats), Supreme Court, media ecosystem, MAGA movement
historicalCrises.approach: Trump: Populist nationalism, 'America First', institutional defiance, false election claims. Opponents: Impeachments, investigations, resistance. Court battles. Social media amplification.
historicalCrises.impact: Democratic backsliding in USA, institutional strain, political violence normalized, global democracy in retreat, US alliances strained, authoritarian leaders emboldened.

importantFigures.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt (FDR)
importantFigures.description: Led USA through Great Depression and WWII. Created New Deal social programs, expanded federal government role. Forged Allied coalition against Axis powers.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Pragmatic liberalism, charismatic communication (fireside chats), coalition-building, willing to expand executive power during crises.
importantFigures.legacy: Welfare state foundation, defeated fascism, US global leadership established, social security, regulatory state, liberal internationalism.

importantFigures.name: Adolf Hitler
importantFigures.description: Led Nazi Party to power through democratic collapse. Initiated WWII, Holocaust (6 million Jews murdered). Totalitarian ideology combined fascism, racism, expansionism.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Authoritarian populism, propaganda mastery, cult of personality, total control through terror, scapegoating, charismatic mass rallies.
importantFigures.legacy: Worst genocide in history, WWII devastation, lesson in democratic fragility, human rights norms created in response, de-Nazification, Germany's pacifist turn.

importantFigures.name: Mao Zedong
importantFigures.role: Communist Party Chairman, Founder of PRC
importantFigures.description: Led Communist revolution, founded People's Republic 1949. Great Leap Forward famine (30M+ deaths), Cultural Revolution chaos. Transformed China into communist state.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Revolutionary ideology, mass mobilization, cult of personality, permanent revolution doctrine, peasant-based communism, purges of rivals.
importantFigures.legacy: Unified China under communism, millions died in famines/purges, laid foundation for modern China despite chaos, Maoism influenced global left, still revered officially.

importantFigures.name: Nelson Mandela
importantFigures.description: Imprisoned 27 years for fighting apartheid. Led peaceful transition to democracy 1994. Nobel Peace Prize. Symbol of reconciliation and moral leadership.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Reconciliation over revenge, moral authority, inclusive nationalism, forgiveness, coalition-building, truth and reconciliation commission.
importantFigures.legacy: Ended apartheid peacefully, model for transitional justice, global icon of freedom, inspirational leadership, South African democracy established (though challenges remain).

importantFigures.name: John F. Kennedy (JFK)
importantFigures.description: Youngest elected president. Cuban Missile Crisis averted nuclear war. Initiated civil rights push, space race. Assassinated 1963 in Dallas. Created Camelot mystique.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Charismatic leadership, inspirational rhetoric ('Ask not what your country...'), crisis management, media-savvy, liberal Cold Warrior.
importantFigures.legacy: Avoided nuclear war, inspired generation, space program, civil rights progress, assassination trauma, idealized memory, Vietnam escalation began under him.

importantFigures.name: Margaret Thatcher
importantFigures.description: First woman PM of UK. Led conservative revolution: privatization, union-busting, free markets. Close ally of Reagan. Falklands War. Transformed British politics.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Conviction politics, uncompromising, neoliberal economics, strong nationalism, confrontational with opponents, individualism over collectivism.
importantFigures.legacy: Neoliberalism implemented, weakened unions, privatized industries, divided legacy (revitalized economy vs increased inequality), shifted Labour Party right.

importantFigures.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
importantFigures.description: Introduced glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring). Ended Cold War peacefully. USSR collapsed under his watch 1991. Nobel Peace Prize.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Reformist communist, dialogue over confrontation, allowed dissent, negotiated arms reductions, refused to use force to save empire.
importantFigures.legacy: Ended Cold War peacefully, Soviet collapse (unintended), freed Eastern Europe, hated in Russia for empire loss, respected in West, democratic reforms enabled.

importantFigures.name: Saddam Hussein
importantFigures.description: Brutal Ba'athist dictator. Iran-Iraq War (1980-88), invaded Kuwait (1990), Gulf War defeat. Used chemical weapons on Kurds. Overthrown in US invasion 2003, executed 2006.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Authoritarian personalism, cult of personality, brutal repression, secret police state, secular Arab nationalism, patronage networks, Sunni minority rule.
importantFigures.legacy: Regional instability, devastating wars, mass graves discovered, Iraq War consequences (sectarian violence, ISIS rise), symbol of Middle East dictatorships.

importantFigures.name: Muammar Gaddafi
importantFigures.description: Seized power in coup 1969. Idiosyncratic ideology ('Third International Theory'), sponsored terrorism, oil wealth. Arab Spring uprising led to NATO intervention, killed 2011.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Erratic personalism, eccentric ideology, direct democracy rhetoric with authoritarian practice, anti-Western but pragmatic, tribal patronage.
importantFigures.legacy: Libya state collapse post-death, civil war, migrant crisis, lesson in intervention failure, Pan-Africanism advocacy, bizarre public persona.

importantFigures.name: Bashar al-Assad
importantFigures.description: Inherited power from father 2000. Arab Spring protests 2011 led to civil war. Used chemical weapons, barrel bombs on civilians. Survived with Russian/Iranian help. 500K+ dead.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Authoritarian survival, brutal repression, sectarian divide-and-rule (Alawite minority), dependent on Russia/Iran, propaganda, siege warfare.
importantFigures.legacy: Massive war crimes, Syria destroyed, refugee crisis, Russian intervention in Middle East, Iran influence, ISIS exploited chaos, ongoing humanitarian catastrophe.

importantFigures.name: Donald Trump
importantFigures.description: Business mogul turned populist politician. 'America First' nationalism, trade wars, two impeachments, COVID mismanagement, election denial, Jan 6 Capitol attack, criminal indictments, reelected 2024.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Populist authoritarianism, norm-breaking, Twitter/social media politics, rally showmanship, media manipulation, personal loyalty over institutions, transactional.
importantFigures.legacy: Democratic backsliding, MAGA movement, reshaped Republican Party, Supreme Court conservatives, US alliances strained, polarization extreme, testing democratic limits.

importantFigures.name: Xi Jinping
importantFigures.description: Most powerful Chinese leader since Mao. Belt and Road Initiative, tech authoritarianism, Xinjiang camps, Hong Kong crackdown, Taiwan threats, zero-COVID. Consolidated power through anti-corruption purges.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Authoritarian consolidation, personality cult ('Xi Jinping Thought'), surveillance state, nationalism, centralization, intolerance of dissent, assertive foreign policy.
importantFigures.legacy: China's rise challenged US, tech authoritarianism model, Uyghur genocide, Belt and Road debt diplomacy, ended term limits (potential leader for life), great power rivalry.

importantFigures.name: Angela Merkel
importantFigures.description: Longest-serving German chancellor. Eurozone crisis manager, refugee crisis (1M+ accepted 2015), Brexit negotiator, climate advocate. Steady leadership during crises.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Pragmatic centrism, consensus-building, cautious incrementalism, scientific rationalism (physicist background), coalition management, non-charismatic but competent.
importantFigures.legacy: EU stabilized, Germany prospered, refugee policy controversial, Russian energy dependence mistake, moral leadership in West, peaceful power transitions.

importantFigures.name: Vladimir Putin
importantFigures.description: Former KGB officer. Centralized power, crushed opposition, enriched oligarchs, annexed Crimea 2014, invaded Ukraine 2022. Nuclear threats, propaganda mastery.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Authoritarian personalism, KGB methods, nationalism, siloviki dominance, media control, political murders, managed democracy facade, great power restoration.
importantFigures.legacy: Destroyed Russian democracy, Ukraine war catastrophe, NATO expansion, isolated Russia, economic stagnation, repression, nuclear brinkmanship, historic miscalculation.

importantFigures.name: Volodymyr Zelensky
importantFigures.description: Former comedian elected 2019 on anti-corruption platform. Became global symbol of resistance when Russia invaded 2022. 'I need ammunition, not a ride' statement iconic. Wartime leadership.
importantFigures.politicalStyle: Communication mastery, media savvy (TV background), moral authority, personal courage (stayed in Kyiv), international lobbying, social media native.
importantFigures.legacy: Ukrainian national unity, inspired democratic resistance, revitalized NATO, exposed Russian weakness, global celebrity, long-term outcome uncertain.

quiz.question: Which concept describes a state's right to control its internal affairs without external interference?
Which theory sees international politics mainly as a struggle for power in an anarchic system?
Which country has the highest military spending in 2026?
Which theory emphasizes that ideas, norms, and identities shape state behavior?
Which conflict is projected to have the most battle deaths in 2026?
What type of power refers to attraction through culture and values rather than coercion?
Which crisis marked the closest the world came to nuclear war during the Cold War?
Which region has the highest democracy score in 2026?
